observations: Ground-mounted commercial/utility-scale solar array visible across an open paddock — multiple rows of panels on steel racking.
Job phase is customer pitch; worker is presenting a solar proposal to a prospective customer.
No worker, customer, or door-knock interaction is visible in the frame — transcript is the primary coaching source.
Array appears well-aligned and in good condition from a distance; no visible damage or soiling.

salesPitchFeedback.message: Good opening using their actual bill figure — that is the right instinct. However, jumping straight to payback period (five years) before the customer has emotionally bought in to the savings can feel transactional. Lead with the monthly saving first: 'That's about $133 a month back in your pocket every single month.' Make them feel it before you frame the payback.

The five-year payback claim is vague and unsubstantiated. Customers will challenge it. Back it up: 'Based on your $400 quarterly bill and current feed-in tariffs in your area, our modelling shows a 4.8-year payback — I can show you that calculation right now.' Specificity builds trust.

Warranty was mentioned but not sold. Saying 'happy to leave the warranty details with you' undersells it. Instead say: 'This system comes with a 25-year panel performance warranty and a 10-year product warranty — that's peace of mind for the next quarter-century, well beyond the payback period.' Make warranty a reason to act, not paperwork to drop off.

The close is weak — 'What questions can I answer?' hands control back to the customer without a clear call to action. Try a soft assumptive close instead: 'I can lock in your site assessment for this week — are mornings or afternoons better for you?' This keeps momentum and moves to the next step.

No mention of government incentives or STCs (Small-scale Technology Certificates). For an Australian residential customer paying $400 a quarter this is a powerful lever: 'On top of the savings, you're also entitled to a federal government rebate through STCs — on a typical system that's worth $2,000 to $4,000 off the upfront cost straight away.' Use it.

Stronger opening line to try next time: 'Based on what you're paying now, a system like the one behind me would save most households around $1,500 a year — that's money that stays in your pocket instead of going to the grid.' Paint the annual figure, it feels bigger and more real than a quarterly comparison.

timeOnTaskNote: Pitch appears to be in early discovery-to-proposal transition. The worker moved to payback and warranty quickly — slow down and spend more time on the customer's pain points and energy usage before presenting numbers. A typical effective door-knock pitch should spend at least 60 seconds on rapport and bill pain before quoting any figures.
nextSteps: Anchor the monthly saving figure clearly before revisiting payback: 'That's roughly $130 a month you'd stop paying the retailer.'
Introduce the STC government rebate immediately — it lowers the perceived upfront cost and creates urgency around current incentive levels.
Move to a clear, time-bound call to action: book a site assessment this week, not an open-ended question about what queries they have.

visualCallouts.label: Show array scale
visualCallouts.message: Point to this large array when talking to the customer — real installations build credibility and help them visualise the outcome on their own property.

visualCallouts.label: Multiple rows — upsell
visualCallouts.message: Multiple rows visible — if the customer has a large property or high bill, use this image to introduce a commercial or larger residential system conversation.

spokenCue.say: Lead with monthly savings, then introduce the government rebate now.
